event: denies exposure to any other medications or chemicals
hyponatremia with a sodium of 124 mmol/L
chest radiograph was remarkable for bilateral lower lobe infiltrates associated with small pleural effusions
transferred to the intensive care unit
acute respiratory failure requiring pressor support and mechanical ventilation
transthoracic echocardiogram showed a large vegetation
transesophageal echocardiogram with contrast study further revealed patent foramen ovale with shunt
CT chest showed multiple cavitary peripheral lung nodules
required renal replacement therapy for acute tubular necrosis
secondary to perfusion-related kidney injury in the setting of septic shock
blood cultures grew methicillin sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA)
oxacillin (2 g IV every 6 h) treatment was initiated
gradually improved on supportive and antimicrobial therapy
eventually weaned off pressors and successfully extubated
skin examination revealed bilateral nontender purpuric papules
localized predominantly on the dorsal aspect of the patient’s hands and feet
evolved into bullous lesions over the following 24–48 hours
similar lesions were noted on the anterior knee and lateral aspect of thighs
she did not have abdominal pain, arthralgias, paresthesia, fever or chills
HIV antibody, hepatitis B serology, p-ANCA and c-ANCA, cryoglobulin and rheumatoid factor, negative
ANA was weakly positive at a titer of 1:40
Complement C3 was low with a level of 14 mg/dL
Anti−HCV antibody was positive with an HCV viral load of 4.16 × 105 IU/mL
No significant changes in red blood count, leukocyte count, and kidney function
Eosinophil count remained within the normal limits throughout the hospital stay
Aspirated fluid from her bullous lesions did not grow bacteria
there were no organisms seen on gram staining
Punch biopsies from the bullous skin lesions showed perivascular neutrophil infiltration with fibrinoid necrosis of small vessels
No bacteria, fungi or evidence of viral inclusions were identified
given the clinical and pathological correlation as well as the temporal relationship
the diagnosis of oxacillin-associated LCV was suspected
vancomycin was started in lieu of oxacillin
resulting in progressive resolution of skin lesions without scar formation
tricuspid valve replacement surgery with no perioperative complications
no recurrence of skin lesions after one month
